lead: Eight weeks sounds like ages until the dates are set and the to-do list starts growing. We have helped Suffolk families move for nearly twenty years, and the smoothest moves almost always follow the same gentle rhythm: a little done each week so nothing piles up at the end. Below is the countdown checklist our own crews would work to, week by week, from the moment a date looks likely right through to the night you finally put the kettle on in your new home.

sections.h: Eight Weeks to Go: Set the Foundations
sections.body:
With roughly two months in hand, the job is all about decisions rather than boxes. Confirm your likely completion date as best you can, and start gathering quotes from removals firms. We always recommend booking a survey at this stage so the quote reflects what you actually own, not a guess. The good dates around month-end and over summer fill up fast, so an early conversation puts you in a far stronger position.

This is also the moment for a proper walk through your home with fresh, ruthless eyes. Note the big items that will need special care, the awkward wardrobe that has to come apart, the loft and shed that always hold more than anyone expects. Making that mental inventory now means nothing surprises you later, and it gives your removals team the detail they need to plan properly.

If you are renting, check your notice period and give it in writing on time. If you are selling, stay in regular contact with your solicitor so you are never the link that holds up the chain. None of this is heavy lifting, but getting the foundations right early makes every week that follows feel calmer.

sections.h: Six Weeks to Go: Declutter and Gather Supplies
sections.body:
Now the practical work begins, and it starts with getting rid of things. There is genuinely no point paying to move what you no longer want, so go room by room and be honest. Charity shops, online selling and a couple of trips to the tip will lighten your load considerably, and in our experience a lighter load often means a shorter, cheaper moving day too.

Tackle the spaces people dread first: the loft, the garage and the shed. These are where the surprises live, and discovering a dozen heavy boxes up a ladder on moving day throws the whole timetable out. Clear them now, while you still have energy and time to deal with whatever you find.

Once the clutter has gone, sort your packing supplies. Order or collect sturdy boxes in a couple of sizes, plenty of tape, bubble wrap, packing paper and marker pens. We would rather you had too many boxes than too few; running out halfway through a Saturday afternoon is a frustration nobody needs. With the house lighter and the materials ready, you are set to pack at a comfortable pace.

sections.h: Four Weeks to Go: Start Packing and Notify People
sections.body:
A month out, begin packing the things you can live without. Out-of-season clothes, books, spare crockery, ornaments and the contents of that loft you have just cleared can all be boxed now. Work room by room, label every box on the side with its destination room and a note of the contents, and keep boxes light enough to lift comfortably. Steady evenings of an hour or two beat a single frantic weekend.

This is also the time to start working through the change-of-address list. Tell the bank, the council, the DVLA, your insurers, your GP and anyone else who writes to you. Set up a postal redirection to catch the things you forget, and make a note of any subscriptions or deliveries that need updating. Spread across a few weeks, this admin barely registers; left to the last minute, it becomes a scramble.

If you have children at school or pets registered with a vet, begin those transfers now as well, since they can take a little time to arrange. The aim by the end of this week is to have the easy rooms largely packed and the paperwork well under way, leaving the final weeks for the things you use every day.

sections.h: Two Weeks to Go: Confirm the Details
sections.body:
With a fortnight left, firm everything up. Confirm the moving date and arrival time with your removals team, and run through any access issues at both ends: narrow Suffolk lanes, a low cottage doorway, double yellow lines, or a new-build where the lorry can only get two doors down. The more we know now, the more smoothly the day itself will run, because we can bring the right vehicle and the right number of crew.

Arrange the utilities. Note which suppliers serve both homes, book final and opening meter readings, and sort the broadband at the new property so you are not without internet for a fortnight. Book any parking suspensions you might need, and give neighbours a friendly heads-up if the lorry will need space outside their house on the morning.

Keep packing steadily through this week, leaving only the kitchen, the beds and daily essentials. Start running down the contents of the freezer too, since defrosting it the day before is far easier than trying to move it full. By the end of this fortnight the house should feel mostly boxed, with just the final everyday items to deal with.

sections.h: The Final Week: Last Jobs and the Essentials Box
sections.body:
The last week is about finishing rather than starting. Pack the remaining rooms, strip everything but the beds you will sleep in, and empty and defrost the fridge and freezer the day before. Dismantle any flat-pack furniture you are confident with, or leave it for our crew if you would rather. Confirm childcare or pet care for the day, so the house is calm and the hallway clear when the lifting starts.

Now pack the one box that matters most: the essentials box. Into it goes the kettle, mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents and a few toys if you have little ones. Keep it in your own car, not on the lorry, and mark it boldly so nobody loads it by mistake. It is the box that keeps everyone going.

Do a final sweep of every cupboard, the loft, the shed and the garden, because it is astonishing what gets left behind in the rush. Locate the keys for the new place and arrange to hand over the old ones. With the checklist complete, the foundations laid weeks ago, you can walk into moving day knowing the hard work is already done.

When the day arrives, the planning pays off. Have the path to the door clear and the lights on, point the crew towards anything fragile or precious, and then let the team get on with the loading. A kettle, mugs and a packet of biscuits left out keeps everyone fuelled and makes for a friendlier day. Take a photo of each meter before you leave, with the date showing, to settle any billing question before it arises.

Before the lorry pulls away, walk through the old home together one last time, checking every space. At the new property, direct boxes to the right rooms as they come off the van, and ask our crew to rebuild the beds before they leave. After a long day, a made-up bed is the difference between a good first night and a miserable one, so make it a priority.

Do not feel you must unpack everything at once. Get the kitchen working enough for breakfast, set up one familiar corner for any children or pets, and leave the rest for the days that follow. The unpacking will still be there in the morning, and after eight weeks of steady, sensible preparation you have earned a proper rest in your new Suffolk home.

faqs: When should I start a moving house checklist?

Around eight weeks before your likely completion date is ideal. It gives you time to book removals, declutter, notify people and pack at a steady pace. The smoothest moves we see are simply the ones that started early and did a little each week.
How early should I book a removals company?

As soon as you have a date you are reasonably confident in, usually around the eight-week mark. Good firms fill up quickly at month-end and over summer, and an early survey means your quote reflects what you actually own rather than a rough guess.

Begin around four weeks out with the things you can live without, such as out-of-season clothes, books and loft contents. Leave the kitchen, beds and daily essentials for the final week, and pack steadily rather than cramming it all into one weekend.
What should I do in the last week before moving?

Finish packing every room, strip all but the beds you will sleep in, defrost the fridge and freezer, and pack an essentials box for your own car. Confirm the date and access with your removals team, and do a final sweep of the loft, shed and cupboards.
